findings.title: group_by_series uses a stricter episode-marker regex than parsing, so S##E## markers wrapped in (), [], or other non-[_\s.\-,] chars are NOT detected, causing real series episodes that also carry a year to be misrouted as movies.
findings.mechanism: Line 578 gates episode parsing on `if not movie_info or _PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER.search(filename)`. grouping._PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER (line 36) is `(?:^|[_\s.\-,])[Ss]\d{1,2}[Ee]\d{1,3}(?:[_\s.\-,]|$)` which only accepts `_ space . - ,` as boundaries, whereas parsing._PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER (parsing.py line 33) uses `\b`. When a filename has a year (so parse_movie_info returns truthy) AND the S##E## is delimited by `(` `[` `:` `/` `+` (e.g. `(S02E03)`), the grouping regex misses it, `not movie_info` is False, the OR short-circuits, and parse_episode_info is never called. The episode falls through to non_series, then group_movies parses it as a movie.
findings.trigger_input: Westworld 2016 (S01E01).mkv, Westworld 2016 (S01E02).mkv, Westworld 2016 (S02E01).mkv (or Dark.2017.(S01E05).mkv, Westworld (2016) [S02E03].mkv)
findings.wrong_output: All episodes collapse into a single movie entry keyed 'westworld|2016' with 3 versions; the entire season/episode structure is destroyed and the title never appears under Series. parse_episode_info would correctly return westworld S02E03 if it had been called.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py line 36: `_PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER = re.compile(r'(?:^|[_\s.\-,])[Ss]\d{1,2}[Ee]\d{1,3}(?:[_\s.\-,]|$)')` — boundaries restricted to `_ space . - ,`, so a `(` or `[` before `S`, or `)`/`]` after the episode number, is NOT accepted.
grouping.py line 573-579 (the gate):
  `movie_info = parse_movie_info(filename)`
  `ep_info = None`
  `if not movie_info or _PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER.search(filename):`
  `    ep_info = parse_episode_info(filename)`
Contrast parsing.py line 21: `_PATTERN_S00E00 = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]+[\(\[]?[Ss](\d{1,2})[Ee](\d{1,3})[\)\]]?')` — parse_episode_info DOES accept optional brackets `[\(\[]?...[\)\]]?`, and parsing.py line 34 `_PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]*[\(\[]?((?:19|20)\d{2})...')` makes parse_movie_info return truthy whenever a 19xx/20xx year precedes the marker. So for a bracketed marker + year, movie_info is truthy AND the grouping marker misses, the OR short-circuits False, and parse_episode_info is never called; the file falls to result['non_series'] (line 685) and group_movies (line 789-795) keys it as a movie.
findings.repro:
Input file list (single series search result):
  'Westworld 2016 (S01E01).mkv', 'Westworld 2016 (S01E02).mkv', 'Westworld 2016 (S02E01).mkv'

Observed (python3, real lib.parsing + grouping's exact line-36 regex):
For each of the three files —
  parse_movie_info -> truthy, title='westworld', year=2016
  grouping _PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER.search(filename) -> False  (because the char before 'S' is '(' and after 'E01' is ')')
  gate `not movie_info or marker.search` -> False  => parse_episode_info NOT called
  yet parse_episode_info(filename) WOULD return ('westworld', S1E1 / S1E2 / S2E1) correctly

Downstream group_movies key construction (line 795 `f\"{title}|{year}\"`):
  movie key 'westworld|2016' -> 3 versions  [all three episodes collapsed]

Expected: 1 series 'westworld' with seasons {1:{1,2}, 2:{1}} (3 unique episodes).
Actual: 0 series; 1 movie 'westworld|2016' with 3 bogus "versions"; episode/season structure destroyed; title never appears under Series.

Also reproduced identically for 'Dark.2017.(S01E05).mkv' (-> movie 'dark|2017') and 'Westworld (2016) [S02E03].mkv' (-> movie 'westworld|2016').
Control 'Westworld 2016 S01E01.mkv' (space-delimited marker): grouping marker matches True -> correctly routed to series, proving the bug is specific to non-[_ space . - ,] delimiters around the S##E## marker.

Fix: align grouping._PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER with parsing (allow optional `[\(\[]` / `[\)\]]` around the marker, or reuse parsing._PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER with \b boundaries / the S00E00 pattern), or simply gate episode parsing on parse_episode_info() result rather than the stricter local regex.

findings.title: Movies titled as a name plus a trailing sequel number with no year (e.g. 'Avatar 2', 'Deadpool 2') are parsed as series episode S01E02 because parse_movie_info returns None and the absolute-episode guard only blocks single words shorter than 6 chars.
findings.mechanism: With no year, parse_movie_info returns None, so group_by_series line 578 runs parse_episode_info. The absolute-episode branch (_PATTERN_ABSOLUTE_EP) matches '<name> 2' with episode=2. The only guard (line 568-572) for non-'ep'/'episode' filenames rejects a single-word series name shorter than 6 chars; multi-word names and single words >=6 chars pass. So 'avatar'(6), 'deadpool', 'gladiator', 'top gun', 'sicario', 'frozen', 'zootopia' all slip through; only short single-word titles like 'Dune 2','Joker 2','Wonka 2','Moana 2' are saved.
findings.trigger_input: Avatar 2.mkv, Deadpool 2.mkv, Gladiator 2.mkv, Top Gun 2.mkv, Sicario 2.mkv, Frozen 2.mkv, Zootopia 2.mkv
findings.wrong_output: 'Avatar 2.mkv' -> {is_series:True, series_name:'avatar', season:1, episode:2}; the movie is listed under Series as Avatar season 1 episode 2 instead of as a movie.
findings.evidence:
parsing.py line 25: `_PATTERN_ABSOLUTE_EP = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s\.\-]+(?:ep?\.?\s*)?(\d{1,3})(?!\d)', re.IGNORECASE)` — matches "Avatar 2" with name="Avatar", episode=2.

parsing.py lines 567-572 (the only non-ep guard):
```
has_ep_marker = 'ep' in cleaned_filename.lower() or 'episode' in cleaned_filename.lower()
if not has_ep_marker:
    words = series_name.split()
    if len(words) == 1 and len(series_name) < 6:
        return None
```
This rejects ONLY single-word names shorter than 6 chars. Multi-word names and single words >=6 chars pass.

grouping.py lines 573-579:
```
movie_info = parse_movie_info(filename)
ep_info = None
if not movie_info or _PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER.search(filename):
    ep_info = parse_episode_info(filename)
```
parse_movie_info returns None when there is no year (`_PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR` requires `(19|20)\d{2}`), so `not movie_info` is True and parse_episode_info runs.
findings.repro:
Ran `python3 -c "from lib.parsing import parse_episode_info; print(parse_episode_info('Avatar 2.mkv'))"`:

'Avatar 2.mkv' -> {'is_series': True, 'series_name': 'avatar', 'season': 1, 'episode': 2, 'original_name': 'Avatar 2.mkv'}  (EXPECTED: a movie, or non_series)
'Deadpool 2.mkv'  -> series 'deadpool' S1E2
'Gladiator 2.mkv' -> series 'gladiator' S1E2
'Top Gun 2.mkv'   -> series 'top gun' S1E2  (multi-word passes guard)
'Sicario 2.mkv'   -> series 'sicario' S1E2
'Frozen 2.mkv'    -> series 'frozen' S1E2   (clean name len==6, passes < 6 guard)
'Zootopia 2.mkv'  -> series 'zootopia' S1E2

Guard saves only short single words (verified clean_series_name lengths: dune=4, joker=5, wonka=5, moana=5):
'Dune 2.mkv'/'Joker 2.mkv'/'Wonka 2.mkv'/'Moana 2.mkv' -> parse_episode_info returns None.

get_display_name keeps the raw filename for these (absolute-ep path), so in the UI "Avatar 2.mkv" appears under Series as display "Avatar 2.mkv", season 1, episode 2 instead of as a movie. Candidate's claimed wrong output is exact.

findings.title: clean_series_name strips any 4-digit token via _PATTERN_YEAR, removing numbers that are an intrinsic part of a real title (Blade Runner 2049, Space 1999, Stranger 1899), producing a wrong canonical key that collides with the un-numbered title.
findings.mechanism: Line 307 applies _PATTERN_YEAR = `\[?\d{4}\]?` (parsing.py line 30) which matches every 4-digit run, not just plausible release years. The restore-guard at line 308-309 only fires when the WHOLE name was a year, so multi-word titles lose the number. 'Blade Runner 2049' -> 'blade runner', 'Space 1999' -> 'space', 'Stranger 1899' -> 'stranger'. This is the canonical key used for series grouping (parse_episode_info line 454/558) and for movie titles (parse_movie_info line 607).
findings.trigger_input: Blade Runner 2049 S01E01.mkv (series) or Blade Runner 2049 1080p (movie raw_title); also Space 1999 S02E03.mkv, Stranger 1899 S01E02.mkv
findings.wrong_output: series_name/canonical key 'blade runner' instead of 'blade runner 2049' — episodes/versions of Blade Runner 2049 (2017) get merged with the original Blade Runner (1982) under the same key.
findings.evidence: parsing.py:30 `_PATTERN_YEAR = re.compile(r'\[?\d{4}\]?')` matches ANY 4-digit run, not just plausible years. clean_series_name strips it at parsing.py:307 `name = _PATTERN_YEAR.sub('', name)`. The restore-guard at parsing.py:308-309 `if not name.strip(): name = name_before_year_strip  # Restore — name was entirely a year` only fires when the WHOLE name reduced to empty, so multi-word titles permanently lose the digit token. This key is the series canonical key: parse_episode_info sets `series_name = clean_series_name(raw_name)` (lines 454, 479, 498, 558) and grouping.py:588 `canonical_key = series` groups episodes by it.
findings.repro:
SERIES (fully confirmed, end-to-end collision):
  parse_episode_info('Blade Runner 2049 S01E01.mkv') -> {'series_name': 'blade runner', ...}
  parse_episode_info('Blade Runner S01E02.mkv')       -> {'series_name': 'blade runner', ...}
  => identical canonical_key 'blade runner' => grouping.py:618 puts both under result['series']['blade runner'], merging episodes of two distinct shows.
  Also: 'Space 1999 S02E03.mkv' -> 'space';  'Stranger 1899 S01E02.mkv' -> 'stranger'.
  Guard check: clean_series_name('1883')->'1883' (restored), but clean_series_name('Blade Runner 2049')->'blade runner' (NOT restored), clean_series_name('Space 1999')->'space'.

MOVIE path (partially confirmed, NOT for the exact 'Blade Runner 2049' example):
  parse_movie_info('Blade Runner 2049 1080p.mkv') -> None  (because _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR at parsing.py:34 captures 2049 as year, rejected as future at line 604 — so no wrong key here).
  BUT confirmed wrong for real cases: parse_movie_info('Space.1999.1975.mkv') -> {'title':'space','year':1999} (intrinsic '1999' stripped from raw_title 'Space' via clean_series_name at line 607), collides movie key 'space|1999' with any film titled just 'Space' from 1999. Likewise 'Stranger.1899.2022.S01E01.mkv' -> title 'stranger'.

findings.title: parse_movie_info's lazy title-then-year regex grabs the FIRST 19xx/20xx token in the name; when that token is a title number (e.g. 'Blade Runner 2049') it is taken as the year, then the future-year guard rejects the whole file, so the movie is never grouped.
findings.mechanism: _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR (parsing.py line 34) = `^(.+?)[\s_.\-]*[\(\[]?((?:19|20)\d{2})(?!x\d{3,4})[\)\]]?`. The lazy `.+?` makes it bind to the earliest 19/20xx run. For 'Blade Runner 2049 1080p BluRay.mkv' it captures year=2049. Line 604 then rejects `year > _CURRENT_YEAR + 2` (2049 > 2028) and returns None. So the file is not a movie; with no episode marker it is also not a series and lands in non_series (loose, ungrouped). For 'Blade Runner 2049 2017.mkv' the real year 2017 is never reached for the same reason.
findings.trigger_input: Blade Runner 2049 1080p BluRay.mkv, Blade Runner 2049 2017.mkv, Death Race 2050 2017.mkv
findings.wrong_output: parse_movie_info returns None; the movie is dropped into result['non_series'] as an ungrouped loose file instead of a movie titled 'Blade Runner 2049'.
findings.evidence: parsing.py line 34: `_PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]*[\(\[]?((?:19|20)\d{2})(?!x\d{3,4})[\)\]]?')` — the lazy `.+?` binds to the EARLIEST `(?:19|20)\d{2}` token. parse_movie_info lines 592-605: `match = _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR.match(filename)` ... `year = int(match.group(2))` ... `if year > _CURRENT_YEAR + 2: return None`. With _CURRENT_YEAR=2026 (line 9, `datetime.datetime.now().year`), the cutoff is 2028. Regex match on 'Blade Runner 2049 1080p BluRay.mkv' gives group(1)='Blade Runner', group(2)='2049' → year 2049 > 2028 → returns None. grouping.py line 685 then appends the file to result['non_series'] (no episode marker, so parse_episode_info also returns None at line 579). group_movies (lines 789-792) re-runs parse_movie_info and does `if not movie_info: continue`, so it is never grouped as a movie and stays loose in non_series.
findings.repro:
Ran `python3 -c "from lib.parsing import parse_movie_info, parse_episode_info; ..."` in repo root. Observed:
'Blade Runner 2049 1080p BluRay.mkv' -> movie: None, episode: None
'Blade Runner 2049 2017.mkv' -> movie: None, episode: None  (real year 2017 never reached; regex stops at 2049)
'Death Race 2050 2017.mkv' -> movie: None, episode: None
'Blade Runner 2049 (2017) 1080p.mkv' -> movie: None, episode: None
Regex internals confirmed: _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR.match('Blade Runner 2049 2017.mkv') -> title='Blade Runner', year='2049' (CURRENT_YEAR=2026, cutoff=2028). Expected: a movie dict {'is_movie': True, 'title': 'Blade Runner 2049', 'year': 2017}. Actual: None → file lands ungrouped in result['non_series']. Control case '2012 2009.mkv' -> correctly {'title':'2012','year':2009} because 2012<=2028, confirming the only failure axis is the future-year guard rejecting a title-number.

findings.title: parse_movie_info extracts a false year from an in-title 19xx/20xx number (not a release year) and truncates the title at that token.
findings.mechanism: Same lazy `.+?` + `(?:19|20)\d{2}` in _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR (parsing.py line 34). The `(?!x\d{3,4})` negative lookahead only blocks resolution forms like '1920x1080', not bare in-title numbers. So a 19xx/20xx number that is part of the title or a description is consumed as the year and everything after it is dropped from raw_title.
findings.trigger_input: Studio 1950 documentary.mkv, Highway 2000 chase.mkv, Documentary about 1984 events.mkv, Race car 2000hp build.mkv
findings.wrong_output: 'Highway 2000 chase.mkv' -> title='highway', year=2000 (loses 'chase' and misdates the film); 'Race car 2000hp build.mkv' -> title='race car', year=2000 — wrong canonical key 'highway|2000' / 'race car|2000'.
findings.evidence:
parsing.py:34 `_PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]*[\(\[]?((?:19|20)\d{2})(?!x\d{3,4})[\)\]]?')` — the only post-year guard is `(?!x\d{3,4})`, which blocks resolution forms like `1920x1080` but NOT bare in-title numbers. The lazy `(.+?)` grabs the shortest prefix, so the FIRST 19xx/20xx token is consumed as the year.
parse_movie_info (parsing.py:592-595): `match = _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR.match(filename)`; `raw_title = match.group(1)`; `year = int(match.group(2))`. raw_title is whatever precedes the first year token; everything after it (including real title words) is dropped.
Remaining guards do not catch this: line 599-600 `alnum_chars < 2` passes ("highway"=7 alnum), line 604 `year > _CURRENT_YEAR + 2` passes (2000<2028). No guard checks for dropped trailing content.
grouping.py:573 calls `parse_movie_info(filename)` BEFORE episode parsing, and line 578 only tries episode parsing `if not movie_info or _PATTERN_EPISODE_MARKER.search(filename)` — so these no-S##E## files are locked in as movies. grouping.py:795 `canonical_key = f"{movie_info['title']}|{movie_info['year']}"` and line 798 `display_name = movie_info['raw_title']` use the truncated values.
findings.repro:
Ran: `python3 -c "from lib.parsing import parse_movie_info; ..."`
Observed:
'Highway 2000 chase.mkv'  -> {'title': 'highway', 'year': 2000, 'raw_title': 'Highway', ...}  (loses 'chase', misdates film; canonical key 'highway|2000')
'Race car 2000hp build.mkv' -> {'title': 'race car', 'year': 2000, 'raw_title': 'Race car', ...}  ('2000hp' read as year 2000; canonical key 'race car|2000')
'Studio 1950 documentary.mkv' -> {'title': 'studio', 'year': 1950, 'raw_title': 'Studio', ...}
'Documentary about 1984 events.mkv' -> {'title': 'documentary about', 'year': 1984, 'raw_title': 'Documentary about', ...}  (truncated mid-phrase)
Expected: title/year should not be extracted from a non-release in-title number; e.g. 'Race car 2000hp build' has no release year and should not yield year=2000 with title='race car'. Actual output truncates the title and emits a wrong canonical_key, causing mis-grouping/dedup and a clipped display name.

findings.title: Series version sort lambda crashes the entire grouping when any file's 'size' is non-numeric text or a list
findings.mechanism: The sort key `int(v.get('size',0)) if v.get('size') else 0` runs on every episode-version list at lines 687-703 (and again at 727-736). `v.get('size')` comes straight from todict() of the API XML <size> child text, which is unvalidated. A non-numeric string (e.g. 'N/A') makes int() raise ValueError; duplicated <size> tags make todict() return a list, making int() raise TypeError; either way it is unhashable for the (name,size) dedup key too. This sort loop is in the main body of group_by_series with NO try/except around it (the try only wraps group_movies at 744-763), so the exception propagates out of group_by_series and aborts the whole search.
findings.trigger_input: A series file whose XML has non-numeric size, e.g. <file><ident>x</ident><name>Show S01E01.mkv</name><size>N/A</size></file>, or duplicated tags <size>100</size><size>200</size> (todict -> size=['100','200']). Realistic with maybe_removed=true entries.
findings.wrong_output: ValueError/TypeError propagates from group_by_series; fetch_and_group_series returns nothing and the entire search result list fails to render — user sees an empty/error screen instead of the matching episodes.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py:697 (series sort, main body, NO try/except around lines 687-736):
    deduplicated.sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size', 0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)
grouping.py:733 (post-merge series sort, same pattern). The 'size' value is unvalidated — it comes from utils.py todict() which assigns raw element text or a LIST on duplicate tags:
    utils.py:131  value = todict(e, skip) if len(e) > 0 else e.text
    utils.py:132-136  if e.tag in result: ... result[e.tag] = [result[e.tag], value]   # duplicate <size> -> list
Files enter via grouping.py:1305  item = todict(file). The only try/except in group_by_series wrap the settings read (line 555) and group_movies (line 745); the series sort loop is unguarded. Caller path is also unguarded: search_ui.py:77 calls fetch_and_group_series outside any try (the try at 73-76 closes before line 77), and routing.py:34 dispatches search() with no surrounding try, so the exception is uncaught and endOfDirectory() is never reached.
findings.repro:
Input: a series file dict {'name':'Show S01E01.mkv','ident':'abc','size':'N/A'} (non-numeric <size> text) OR {'size':['100','200']} (duplicated <size> tags -> todict list).
parse_episode_info('Show S01E01.mkv') => {'is_series':True,'season':1,'episode':1,...} so it enters the series branch and reaches the sort.
Ran the exact lambda:
  >>> [{'size':'N/A'}].sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size',0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)
  ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'N/A'
  >>> [{'size':['100','200']}].sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size',0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)
  TypeError: int() argument must be ... not 'list'
Expected: the bad file is skipped/sorted as 0 and the rest of the matching episodes render. Actual: exception propagates out of group_by_series -> fetch_and_group_series -> router; the search directory aborts before endOfDirectory(), so the user gets an empty/error screen with zero results instead of the matching episodes. (Empty/None <size> is safe: the `if v.get('size')` guard short-circuits to 0 — verified, no crash.)

findings.title: parse_movie_info(file_dict['name']) uses direct subscript, so a file dict with no 'name' key kills all movie grouping for the whole result set
findings.mechanism: Line 789 does `parse_movie_info(file_dict['name'])` (not .get). In group_by_series a file with empty/missing name is routed to non_series via `file_dict.get('name','')` (line 566-568), preserving a dict that has NO 'name' key. That same dict is then passed to group_movies via result['non_series'] (line 746). On the first such file, file_dict['name'] raises KeyError, which is caught by the outer try (761) — so group_movies never returns and result['movies'] stays empty: every legitimate movie in the search collapses into the ungrouped non_series flat list.
findings.trigger_input: Any file element without a <name> child alongside real movies, e.g. <file><ident>abc</ident><size>100</size></file> (todict -> {'ident':'abc','size':'100'}).
findings.wrong_output: All movies are shown ungrouped as individual raw files (no 'Title (Year)' folders) — movie grouping silently disabled for that entire search.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py line 788-792 (group_movies loop):
    for file_dict in files:
        movie_info = parse_movie_info(file_dict['name'])   # <-- direct subscript, KeyError if no 'name'
        if not movie_info:
            continue

The nameless dict is admitted to non_series in group_by_series (line 566-569):
        filename = file_dict.get('name', '')
        if not filename:  # Handle empty/None filenames
            result['non_series'].append(file_dict)   # <-- preserves dict that lacks 'name'
            continue

and that list is fed straight into group_movies (line 746) inside the outer try (line 745) / except (line 761):
            movies_result = group_movies(result['non_series'])
            result['movies'] = movies_result['movies']   # line 747 — never runs once line 746 raises
        ...
        except Exception as e:
            log_debug(f"Error grouping movies: {e}")

result is initialized at line 563 as {'series': {}, 'movies': {}, 'non_series': []}, so on the KeyError result['movies'] stays {}.

todict (lib/utils.py:122-139) builds the dict only from child tags present:
        for e in xml:
            ... result[e.tag] = value
so a <file> with no <name> child yields a dict with no 'name' key. parse_movie_info has no internal guard for a missing key — it is the caller's subscript that fails.
findings.repro:
Trigger: a Webshare search response containing at least one <file> element with no <name> child alongside real movies, e.g. <file><ident>abc</ident><size>100</size></file>.

Ran todict logic on that element -> {'ident': 'abc', 'size': '100'} ; "'name' in dict: False". group_by_series line 566 .get('name','') yields '' -> dict appended to non_series unchanged. group_movies line 789 file_dict['name'] -> observed: KeyError('name').

Ran the group_movies inner loop on files=[Inception.2010 (has name), nameless dict, Avatar.2009 (has name)]:
  observed: "Loop ABORTED with KeyError on KeyError('name') -> result['movies'] partial: ['inception|2010']".
Because the exception escapes group_movies before its return (line 853), movies_result is never bound; line 747 assignment is skipped, caught at line 761. The internally-built movie dict is discarded.

Expected: result['movies'] = {'inception|2010': {...}, 'avatar|2009': {...}} (two grouped 'Title (Year)' folders), and non_series filtered to drop the grouped idents.
Actual: result['movies'] == {} and result['non_series'] still holds ALL files (lines 755-758 also skipped). Every legitimate movie is displayed as an individual raw filename — movie grouping silently disabled for the entire search.

Fix: use parse_movie_info(file_dict.get('name','')) at line 789 (and guard the .get('ident') subscript at line 753/757), matching the .get convention used everywhere else.

findings.title: _RE_TRAILING_NUM strips legitimate sequel/season numbers from the display name (e.g. 'Cobra Kai 3' -> 'Cobra Kai', 'Rocky 4' -> 'Rocky')
findings.mechanism: clean_name() applies _RE_TRAILING_NUM = `[-\s]+\d{1,3}(...)?...$` which removes any trailing 1-3 digit number (optionally followed by serie/season/dab). For series/movie names where the trailing number is part of the title (sequel number, season label), the number is deleted. Because the function returns best_cleaned (the cleaned string), the user-facing label loses the distinguishing number.
findings.trigger_input: display_name candidates such as 'Cobra Kai 3', 'Rocky 4', 'Most 2 serie', 'Rocky 4 dab' (e.g. one episode's get_display_name output ending in a season/part number).
findings.wrong_output: 'Cobra Kai 3' -> 'Cobra Kai'; 'Rocky 4' -> 'Rocky'; 'Most 2 serie' -> 'Most' (number dropped, distinct seasons/sequels collapse visually).
findings.evidence:
lib/grouping.py:46 — `_RE_TRAILING_NUM = re.compile(r'[-\s]+\d{1,3}(?:\.\d+)?(\s+(serie|série|season|sezona|disk))?\s*(dab|BEZ HESLA)?$', re.IGNORECASE)` matches ANY trailing 1-3 digit number, the optional serie/season suffix being just that — optional.
lib/grouping.py:389 — `cleaned = _RE_TRAILING_NUM.sub('', cleaned)` inside `clean_name()`, unconditionally strips it.
lib/grouping.py:421,425 — the function returns the CLEANED string: `best_cleaned = sorted_names[0][0]` ... `return best_cleaned`, so the stripped (number-less) form becomes the user-facing label.

pick_best_display_name_from_list(['Cobra Kai 3','Cobra Kai 3','Cobra Kai 3']) -> 'Cobra Kai'
pick_best_display_name_from_list(['Most 2 serie']) -> 'Most'

findings.title: Two files with DIFFERENT valid idents but identical (name,size) are deduped, dropping a live mirror in favor of a possibly-dead one
findings.mechanism: After the ident check passes (distinct idents both added to seen_idents), the code still falls through to the name+size fallback because `is_duplicate` is only set on an ident COLLISION, not on a successful distinct-ident insert. So for the 2nd file with a new ident but a (name,size) already seen, `key in seen_name_size` is True and it is dropped. The fallback should be reached only when ident is absent/unknown, but it runs whenever the ident did not collide. WebShare commonly has the same scene release re-uploaded by multiple users (same filename + byte size, different ident); the first-seen one wins regardless of whether it is removed/dead.
findings.trigger_input: versions=[{'ident':'DEAD','name':'Show.S01E01.1080p.mkv','size':'5000'},{'ident':'LIVE','name':'Show.S01E01.1080p.mkv','size':'5000'}]
findings.wrong_output: Returns only the 'DEAD' entry; the working 'LIVE' mirror is silently removed, so the only playable copy can be lost.
findings.repro:
Trigger filename re-uploaded by two users with identical name+byte size, distinct idents:
versions=[{'ident':'DEAD','name':'Show.S01E01.1080p.mkv','size':'5000'},{'ident':'LIVE','name':'Show.S01E01.1080p.mkv','size':'5000'}]
Ran inlined deduplicate_versions (pure logic, no xbmc):
INPUT idents: ['DEAD', 'LIVE']
OUTPUT idents: ['DEAD']   <-- expected ['DEAD','LIVE'] (two distinct files); 'LIVE' silently removed
Control cases confirm isolation: distinct idents + DIFFERENT size -> both kept (['A','B']); same ident -> 2nd dropped (correct); no ident + same name+size -> 2nd dropped (intended fallback). Only the distinct-ident + identical-(name,size) case is wrong. Fix: track had_valid_ident in the loop and skip the name+size fallback when an ident was present, e.g. `if not is_duplicate and not (ident and ident != 'unknown'):`.

findings.title: offset advances by `limit` instead of by `len(page_files)`, so any partial mid-stream page silently skips the unreturned files (missing episodes).
findings.mechanism: After collecting `page_files`, the loop unconditionally does `offset += limit` (1331). It assumes every page returned exactly `limit` files. If the Webshare API returns fewer than `limit` mid-stream (server-side page cap, `maybe_removed` server filtering, throttling) while `total` is still far ahead, the next request starts at `offset+limit` — leaving the range `[offset+len(page_files), offset+limit)` never requested. E.g. first page (offset=0) gives 25, loop request at offset=25/limit=500 returns only 200 (server cap), then offset jumps to 525, so result indices 225..524 (300 files) are never fetched. The fix is `offset += len(page_files)`.
findings.trigger_input: Any large series where Webshare returns a partial page mid-stream, e.g. a popular show with total=5000 where the server caps a page at ~200 results: `what='friends'` returning 5000 hits with one mid-stream page of 200.
findings.wrong_output: ~300 files (whole blocks of episodes/versions) absent from the grouped result; affected seasons/episodes simply do not appear in the series view.
findings.evidence:
lib/grouping.py lines 1302-1332:
  1303  page_files = []
  1304  for file in xml.iter('file'):
  1305      item = todict(file)
  1306      page_files.append(item)
  ...
  1311  all_files.extend(page_files)
  ...
  1314  if len(page_files) < limit * 0.1:   # early-stop only when <50 (limit=500), twice
  1315      consecutive_short += 1
  1316      if consecutive_short >= 2:
  1317          ... break
  ...
  1328  if offset + limit >= total:
  1329      break
  1330
  1331  offset += limit      # <-- advances by `limit`, NOT len(page_files)
  1332  page += 1
The next request uses offset+limit even though only len(page_files) results were actually returned. Compare the first-page path (line 1272 `offset = len(first_page_files)`) which correctly uses actual count — the loop body does not. Caller lib/cache.py:156 invokes with no first_page_files (offset=0, page=0), so the loop runs from iteration 1; caller lib/search_ui.py:78 passes only the first page, so every subsequent loop page is still affected.
findings.repro:
Trigger: what='friends', server returns total=5000; the request at offset=0/limit=500 returns only 200 files (server-side page cap / maybe_removed filtering / throttle), all later requests full. The early-stop guard does NOT fire (200 is not < limit*0.1 = 50). Then `offset += limit` jumps offset 0 -> 500, so result-indices 200..499 are never requested.

Ran the loop arithmetic standalone (parsing/grouping internals not needed — the skip is pure offset math):
  python3 simulation of lines 1279-1332 with server(offset=0)->200 files, else 500, total=5000
  => fetched count: 4700
  => missing count: 300
  => missing indices: 200,201,...,498,499  (exactly the [len(page_files), limit) gap)
Expected: all 5000 files fetched. Actual: 4700 fetched, 300 silently dropped — those episodes/versions never appear in the grouped series view. Fix: change line 1331 to `offset += len(page_files)`.

findings.title: Early-stop on 2 consecutive short pages aborts fetching while `total` indicates many results remain, dropping the bulk of episodes.
findings.mechanism: `if len(page_files) < limit*0.1: consecutive_short += 1; if >= 2: break` (1314-1318). With default limit=500 the threshold is 50. Whenever the server hands back two consecutive pages of <50 files while still mid-stream (server page caps, relevance/`maybe_removed` filtering, rate limiting), the loop breaks before reaching the genuine later pages. The `total` value is ignored once this fires — the break does not check `all_files` count vs `total`. Combined with the line-1331 advance-by-limit bug, two 30-file pages stop the fetch at all=85 when total=5000.
findings.trigger_input: A broad series query whose server pages are partially filled, e.g. `what` with a long tail of fewer-than-50 matches per 500-window (common when `maybe_removed='true'` and the server trims per page).
findings.wrong_output: Fetch terminates with all_files≈85 of total=5000; ~98% of episodes/versions missing from the series list, with only a LOGDEBUG line and no user-visible warning.
findings.evidence:
lib/grouping.py:1313-1331 — the early-stop break fires BEFORE total is read and never compares all_files to total:
  1313        # Early stopping: if pages are returning very few results, stop
  1314        if len(page_files) < limit * 0.1:
  1315            consecutive_short += 1
  1316            if consecutive_short >= 2:
  1317                log_debug('fetch_and_group_series: early stop (diminishing results)')
  1318                break          # <-- breaks here, total parsed only at 1324
  ...
  1324            total = int(xml.find('total').text)
  ...
  1328        if offset + limit >= total:
  1329            break
  1330        offset += limit
The candidate's structural claim is literally true: the break ignores total. limit defaults to 500 (line 1247) so the threshold is 50. page_files is the raw server <file> count with NO client-side filtering (lib/grouping.py:1302-1311 + lib/utils.py:122-139 todict drops nothing), so a "short page" means the server itself returned <50 files. The break only logs LOGDEBUG (1317), no user-visible warning, matching the candidate. HOWEVER the trigger premise (server under-fills offset/limit windows mid-stream) is unverified: pagination is standard offset/limit against Webshare's search API (lib/api.py:43-48), where windows are full until the genuine last page. maybe_removed='true' (1292) ADDS removed files rather than trimming; rate limiting returns None/non-OK hitting the breaks at 1296/1300, not a short page.
findings.repro: Simulated the exact loop (1263-1332). Under STANDARD offset/limit semantics (pages full until last) the early-stop NEVER fires prematurely: total=5000,firstpage=25 -> all_files=5000 ('total-reached'); total=1000 -> 1000; total=26 -> 26. So no episodes are dropped under normal API behavior. ONLY under the candidate's unverified under-fill premise does the harm appear: modeling the server returning 30 files per 500-window with total=5000, firstpage=25 -> all_files=85 ('EARLY-STOP') — exactly the claimed all=85. With no first page -> 60 ('EARLY-STOP'). So the magnitude claim is reproducible IFF the server under-fills mid-stream windows, which the offset/limit contract does not exhibit and I cannot reproduce offline. No concrete real filename/query was found that forces a 30-file mid-stream window from the live API; 'what' is just an illustrative guess. Net: code mechanism real (break ignores total), but trigger rare/unverified -> PLAUSIBLE not CONFIRMED. Suggested fix: gate the break on a total comparison, e.g. only early-stop when len(all_files) >= total or after reading total, instead of breaking before total is even parsed.

findings.title: max_pages cap silently truncates very large result sets at ~10000 files with no user signal.
findings.mechanism: `while page < max_pages` with default max_pages=20 and limit=500 caps the fetch at roughly 500*20 = 10000 files. When `total` exceeds this, the loop hits the page cap and exits via the `while` condition. No `popinfo`/notification is emitted (only the API path logs); the user sees a series view that is missing every file beyond the cap and has no indication the list is incomplete.
findings.trigger_input: A browse/empty query or huge franchise, e.g. `what=NONE_WHAT` (browse) with sort='recent' on category 'video', or `what='star wars'` returning >10000 hits.
findings.wrong_output: Series/movie groups built from only the first ~10000 files; later episodes/titles silently omitted with no warning to the user.
findings.evidence: lib/grouping.py:1247 default args: `def fetch_and_group_series(token, what, category, sort, limit=500, max_pages=20, ...)`. Loop at 1279: `while page < max_pages:`. Per-iteration advance at 1331-1332: `offset += limit` then `page += 1`. The only natural completion exit is 1328-1329: `if offset + limit >= total: break`. There is NO popinfo/notification on hitting the cap — `grouping.py` does not even import popinfo (grep of the module shows only `import xbmc/xbmcaddon` and `log_debug/log_error`). The function just returns `group_by_series(all_files, ...)` at 1335 regardless of whether `total` was exhausted. Both callers pass the default max_pages: search_ui.py:77 `fetch_and_group_series(token, what, category, sort, first_page_files=files, first_page_total=first_total)` and cache.py:156 `fetch_and_group_series(token, params['what'], category, sort_val)`. Neither caller compares fetched count vs the API `total` to detect truncation (display_series_list's `total_items` is the grouped UI-item count, not the API total).
findings.repro: Simulated the exact loop arithmetic (full 500-file pages, no early-stop since 500 is not < limit*0.1=50). Threshold test output: total=10000 -> (10000 files, truncated_by_cap=False, natural exit); total=10001 -> (10000 files, truncated_by_cap=True); total=50000 -> (10000 files, truncated_by_cap=True). So for any API `total` >= 10001 (e.g. browse with what=NONE_WHAT category='video' sort='recent', where Webshare's index returns far more than 10000 hits, or a mega-franchise query), the loop exits via `while page < max_pages` after exactly 20 pages = 10000 files. Expected: all `total` matching files grouped (or at least a user notice "results incomplete"). Actual: only first 10000 files grouped; the remaining total-10000 files (e.g. 40000 for a 50000-result browse) are silently dropped — series/movies built only from the head of the result set, with zero notification. Confirmed in both call paths (page starts at 1 when first_page_files supplied via search_ui, at 0 via cache.py; both cap at 10000).

findings.title: Films whose title ends in a future-looking 4-digit number get rejected entirely instead of treating the number as part of the title.
findings.mechanism: _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR greedily matches the LAST trailing 4-digit run as the 'year'. For 'Blade Runner 2049' it captures group(1)='Blade Runner', group(2)='2049'. The guard `if year > _CURRENT_YEAR + 2: return None` (2049 > 2028) bails out with no fallback to re-interpret 2049 as part of the title or to look for an earlier real year. The function returns None.
findings.trigger_input: 'Blade Runner 2049 BluRay 1080p.mkv', 'Death Race 2050 2017 WEBRip.mkv'
findings.wrong_output: parse_movie_info returns None; group_by_series dumps the file into non_series ungrouped (not shown as a movie). All copies of Blade Runner 2049 scatter as loose files instead of one merged movie entry.
findings.evidence:
lib/parsing.py:34  `_PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]*[\(\[]?((?:19|20)\d{2})(?!x\d{3,4})[\)\]]?')`  — `(.+?)` is non-greedy, so group(2) locks onto the FIRST `(19|20)\d{2}` run in the name.
lib/parsing.py:604  `if year > _CURRENT_YEAR + 2:` then `return None` — bails out with NO fallback to re-scan for a later/real year token.
lib/grouping.py:573-578  movie_info=None → `if not movie_info or ...:` runs parse_episode_info (also None for these); falls through to line 685 `result['non_series'].append(file_dict)`.
lib/grouping.py:789-792  group_movies re-calls `parse_movie_info` → None → `continue`, so the file is never grouped as a movie and stays loose in non_series.
findings.repro:
_CURRENT_YEAR=2026, guard cutoff=2028.
Input 'Blade Runner 2049 BluRay 1080p.mkv': regex group(1)='Blade Runner', group(2)='2049'; 2049>2028 → parse_movie_info returns None. parse_episode_info also returns None (2049>999 absolute-ep cap). Expected: MOVIE title='blade runner 2049' (or grouped under one key). Actual: dumped to non_series ungrouped.
Worse case 'Blade.Runner.2049.2017.1080p.BluRay.x264.mkv': a REAL year 2017 is present, but non-greedy match picks group(2)='2049' first; 2049>2028 → returns None despite 2017 being available. Verified via python3: parse_movie_info(...) -> None for all of: 'Blade Runner 2049 BluRay 1080p.mkv', 'Death Race 2050 2017 WEBRip.mkv' (group(2)='2050'), 'Soldier 2098 2017 BluRay.mkv'. All copies scatter as loose files (no merge, no dedup, no quality ranking). Note: candidate said regex grabs the LAST run — it actually grabs the FIRST (non-greedy); this makes the no-fallback worse.

findings.title: Inline ' a ' / ' the ' removal collapses distinct titles, and is especially destructive for Czech/Slovak where 'a' means 'and'.
findings.mechanism: After lowercasing, `name.replace(' the ',' ').replace(' a ',' ').replace(' an ',' ')` unconditionally deletes any standalone 'a'/'an'/'the' token anywhere in the title. In Czech 'a' is the conjunction 'and', so 'Tom a Jerry'->'tom jerry', 'Lilo a Stitch'->'lilo stitch', 'Máša a medvěd'->'masa medved'. English 'King a Queen'->'king queen', 'Cosmos a Spacetime Odyssey'->'cosmos spacetime odyssey'. This both mangles display-key derivation and can collide unrelated titles.
findings.trigger_input: 'Tom a Jerry S01E01.mkv', 'Lilo a Stitch S01E01.mkv', 'Masa a medved S01E01.mkv'
findings.wrong_output: canonical key 'tom jerry' (loses the 'a'); a title that legitimately is 'Tom and Jerry' (czech 'Tom a Jerry') becomes indistinguishable from a hypothetical 'Tom Jerry', and the 'and' relationship is destroyed in grouping/dedup.
findings.evidence:
lib/parsing.py:338 in clean_series_name unconditionally strips standalone 'a'/'an'/'the' tokens anywhere in the title:

    # Remove inline articles
    name = name.replace(' the ', ' ').replace(' a ', ' ').replace(' an ', ' ')
    name = ' '.join(name.split())

In Czech/Slovak 'a' is the conjunction 'and', so this deletes a meaningful word. lib/grouping.py:583-588 then uses this output verbatim as the canonical grouping/dedup key:

    series = ep_info['series_name']
    ...
    canonical_key = series
findings.repro:
Ran lib/parsing.clean_series_name directly:
  'Tom a Jerry'                -> 'tom jerry'   (loses 'a')
  'Lilo a Stitch'              -> 'lilo stitch'
  'Masa a medved'              -> 'masa medved'
  'King a Queen'               -> 'king queen'
  'Cosmos a Spacetime Odyssey' -> 'cosmos spacetime odyssey'
  'Tom Jerry'                  -> 'tom jerry'   (IDENTICAL to 'Tom a Jerry')

End-to-end through lib.grouping.group_by_series (via tests/conftest mocks), input files:
  [{'name':'Tom a Jerry S01E01.mkv',...}, {'name':'Tom Jerry S01E02.mkv',...}]
Output: a SINGLE series bucket
  CANONICAL KEYS: ['tom jerry']
  key='tom jerry' display='Tom Jerry' seasons={1: [1, 2]}
i.e. the distinct titles "Tom a Jerry" and "Tom Jerry" merge into one series, both episodes pooled under S01, and the merged group is even mislabeled "Tom Jerry".

Refinement vs the candidate's claim: the user-visible DISPLAY name is NOT mangled in isolation — get_display_name(original_name) preserves 'Tom a Jerry' (verified: 'Tom a Jerry S01E01.mkv' -> 'Tom a Jerry'). The damage is confined to the canonical key: (1) conjunction lost in key, (2) collision merge of two genuinely distinct titles differing only by the conjunction. Expected key 'tom a jerry'; actual 'tom jerry'.

findings.title: Leading-article stripping eats real title words for short titles, collapsing distinct series into one canonical key.
findings.mechanism: Unconditional prefix strip: startswith('the ')->[4:], ('a ')->[2:], ('an ')->[3:]. There is no check that a meaningful remainder exists or that the article is truly an article. 'A Team'->'team', 'A-Team'->'team' (the dash is normalized to space by _PATTERN_SEPARATORS before stripping), 'An American Tail'->'american tail' (collides with a series literally named 'American Tail'), 'A-ha'->'ha', 'The The'->'the'. 'A.I. Artificial Intelligence'->'i artificial intelligence'.
findings.trigger_input: 'A Team S01E01.mkv' vs 'The Team S01E01.mkv'; 'An American Tail 1986.mkv' vs 'American Tail 1986.mkv'; 'A-ha S01E01.mkv'
findings.wrong_output: clean_series_name('A Team')=='team'==clean_series_name('The Team'); two unrelated series merge into one canonical key 'team'. 'A-ha' band/series becomes 'ha'.
findings.evidence:
parsing.py lines 323-329 (unconditional, unguarded prefix strip):
```
# Strip articles from START
if name.startswith('the '):
    name = name[4:]
elif name.startswith('a '):
    name = name[2:]
elif name.startswith('an '):
    name = name[3:]
```
The dash is normalized to a space FIRST by `_PATTERN_SEPARATORS = re.compile(r'[\-_\.\,\:\;]+')` (line 32) applied at line 310 `name = _PATTERN_SEPARATORS.sub(' ', name)`, so 'A-Team'/'A-ha' become 'a team'/'a ha' before the strip.

grouping.py line 588 `canonical_key = series` (series = ep_info['series_name'] = cleaned name) — the cleaned name IS the dedup bucket key. Line 795 `canonical_key = f"{movie_info['title']}|{movie_info['year']}"` for movies.

No guard verifies the article is truly an article or that a meaningful remainder survives — any title whose first whitespace-delimited token is "a"/"an"/"the" loses it.
findings.repro:
RAN: `python3 -c "from lib.parsing import clean_series_name, parse_episode_info, parse_movie_info; ..."`

Direct clean_series_name observed:
'A Team' -> 'team' ; 'The Team' -> 'team' ; 'A-Team' -> 'team' ; 'An American Tail' -> 'american tail' ; 'American Tail' -> 'american tail' ; 'A-ha' -> 'ha' ; 'The The' -> 'the' ; 'A.I. Artificial Intelligence' -> 'i artificial intelligence'

Through real entry point parse_episode_info (series_name = canonical key):
'A Team S01E01.mkv'  -> series_name='team'
'The Team S01E01.mkv'-> series_name='team'   (COLLISION: two unrelated shows merge)
'A-Team S01E01.mkv'  -> series_name='team'
'A-ha S01E01.mkv'    -> series_name='ha'

Through parse_movie_info (title|year = canonical key):
'An American Tail 1986.mkv' -> title='american tail'  => key 'american tail|1986'
'American Tail 1986.mkv'    -> title='american tail'  => key 'american tail|1986'  (COLLISION)

Expected vs actual: expected 'a team' and 'the team' to map to distinct canonical keys (e.g. 'a team' vs 'the team', or at least non-equal). Actual: both == 'team', so grouping.py builds a single result['series']['team'] bucket pooling episodes/versions from two different shows. Same for 'An American Tail' colliding with a real series/film literally named 'American Tail'. The A-ha band/series collapses to 'ha'.

findings.title: The ' - ' (dash-with-spaces) branch treats 'Series - EpisodeTitle' as a dual-name pair, fragmenting one series into many canonical keys.
findings.mechanism: When raw_name contains ' - ', it splits into (name1,name2) and returns them as dual names unless name2 looks like an episode number/marker/quality/year. A descriptive episode title ('A Study in Pink', 'The Blind Banker', 'Winter') passes all the false-positive filters, so it is accepted as a second name. In group_by_series the raw_name is taken from the S00E00 match and passed to clean_series_name, which never strips text after the ' - ', so each episode's descriptive suffix lands in the canonical key.
findings.trigger_input: 'Sherlock - A Study in Pink S01E01.mkv', 'Sherlock - The Blind Banker S01E02.mkv', 'Sherlock S01E03.mkv'
findings.wrong_output: Three canonical keys: 'sherlock study in pink', 'sherlock blind banker', 'sherlock'. The series is split into 3 separate entries instead of one; merge passes can't reunite them (no subset/word-order relationship between 'study in pink' and 'blind banker').
findings.evidence: lib/parsing.py clean_series_name strips markers/quality/etc but only collapses ' - ' to a space (line 310 `name = _PATTERN_SEPARATORS.sub(' ', name)` where `_PATTERN_SEPARATORS = re.compile(r'[\-_\.\,\:\;]+')`); it never drops text after the dash. lib/parsing.py extract_dual_names lines 186-217: the ' - ' branch returns (name1,name2) when name2 is not an episode-num/marker/quality/year — a descriptive title passes all four filters (is_episode_num/is_episode_marker/is_quality/is_year all False). In lib/grouping.py group_by_series line 588 `canonical_key = series` (=`ep_info['series_name']` from `clean_series_name(_PATTERN_S00E00.group(1))`, line 454 in parsing). Observed: `parse_episode_info('Sherlock - A Study in Pink S01E01.mkv')['series_name'] == 'sherlock study in pink'`; `extract_dual_names('Sherlock - A Study in Pink') == ('Sherlock','A Study in Pink')`.
findings.repro: Run: `cd /Users/ondrejkminek/Documents/plugin.video.yeplaya && python3 -c "from lib.parsing import parse_episode_info as p; print([p(f)['series_name'] for f in ['Sherlock - A Study in Pink S01E01.mkv','Sherlock - The Blind Banker S01E02.mkv','Sherlock - The Great Game S01E03.mkv']])"` → actual: ['sherlock study in pink','sherlock blind banker','sherlock great game']; expected: all 'sherlock'. CORRECTED TRIGGER (candidate's own 3-file set is partially wrong): the candidate included a bare 'Sherlock S01E03.mkv' (canonical 'sherlock'). With that file present, merge_substring_series (grouping.py L137-153) DOES reunite them: {'sherlock'} is a subset of both suffix keys, 'sherlock' is 8 chars so it passes the single-short-word guard (L134 needs <6 to skip), and the sequel-marker guard (L151) needs min total_episodes>=3 (here 1) so it does not block → all merge into 'sherlock'. The REAL trigger is the common case where EVERY episode carries a descriptive title and no bare-name episode exists, e.g. ['Sherlock - A Study in Pink S01E01.mkv','Sherlock - The Blind Banker S01E02.mkv','Sherlock - The Great Game S01E03.mkv','Sherlock - A Scandal in Belgravia S02E01.mkv','Sherlock - The Hounds of Baskerville S02E02.mkv'] → 5 separate 1-episode "series": 'sherlock study in pink','sherlock blind banker','sherlock great game','sherlock scandal in belgravia','sherlock hounds of baskerville'. No merge pass reunites: multi-word keys, none a subset of another (verified, no subset relationship); word-order merge needs identical word-set (none); similarity merge needs ratio>0.85 (measured 0.54-0.65). The series fragments into one entry per episode.

findings.title: Substring matching `any(qw in name_lower)` keeps unrelated files and, worse, drops legitimately-matching files whose name uses punctuation the query lacks.
findings.mechanism: Relevance is decided by raw substring containment of a query stem inside the cleaned filename. Two failure modes: (1) false keep — query 'her' matches 'mother', 'thor' matches 'author', 'king' matches 'drinking'; (2) false DROP — query 'csi' does NOT substring-match 'c.s.i. new york' (dots break the run), so when at least one other file DOES match (e.g. 'CSI Vegas'), the dotted/punctuated legitimate result is silently removed. The `return filtered if filtered else files` fallback only saves the case where ALL files fail.
findings.trigger_input: query='csi' over files ['C.S.I. New York S01E01.mkv','CSI Vegas S01E01.mkv']
findings.wrong_output: 'C.S.I. New York' is dropped from the result while 'CSI Vegas' survives; user searching 'csi' loses the punctuated variant. Conversely query 'her' retains every 'Mother'/'Brother' file as relevant.
findings.evidence:
lib/grouping.py:96-105 decides relevance by raw substring containment, not word overlap:
  name_lower = _unidecode_filter(stripped).lower()
  # Keep if any query word/stem appears as substring in filename
  if any(qw in name_lower for qw in stems):
      filtered.append(f)
  ...
  return filtered if filtered else files
The stems set is the query split on whitespace (line 73), so 'csi' is one stem. unidecode/lower does NOT strip dots, so 'C.S.I. New York' lowercases to 'c.s.i. new york', and 'csi' is not a contiguous substring of it. The fallback `return filtered if filtered else files` (line 105) only triggers when filtered is EMPTY; here 'CSI Vegas' matches, so filtered is non-empty and the dotted file is permanently dropped. The docstring at lines 67-72 ("word-overlap check... only drops files that have ZERO overlap") contradicts the actual substring implementation. Same substring logic causes false keeps: 'her' is a substring of 'mother'/'brotherhood', 'thor' of 'the author'.
findings.repro:
Ran against the REAL lib.grouping._filter_irrelevant (xbmc stubbed):
  _filter_irrelevant([{'name':'C.S.I. New York S01E01.mkv'},{'name':'CSI Vegas S01E01.mkv'}], 'csi')
  => ['CSI Vegas S01E01.mkv']   # expected both; 'C.S.I. New York' silently dropped
  _filter_irrelevant([{'name':'Mother S01E01.mkv'},{'name':'Brotherhood S01E02.mkv'}], 'her')
  => ['Mother S01E01.mkv','Brotherhood S01E02.mkv']   # both falsely kept (expected: none relevant to 'her')
  _filter_irrelevant([{'name':'The Author S01E01.mkv'},{'name':'Thor 2011.mkv'}], 'thor')
  => ['The Author S01E01.mkv','Thor 2011.mkv']   # 'The Author' falsely kept
False DROP needs query>=3 chars (line 76) and the dotted file to coexist with >=1 clean-matching sibling (so fallback at line 105 doesn't rescue it). Same pattern affects acronym titles: S.W.A.T., F.B.I., S.H.I.E.L.D.

findings.title: Dual-name canonical key silently differs depending on whether the optional csfd_scraper module is importable, splitting one series into two when it is absent.
findings.mechanism: When DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE is True, a dual-name episode like 'The Penguin - Tucnak S01E03' gets canonical_key = create_canonical_from_dual_names(...) = 'penguin|tucnak' (pipe-joined, sorted). When csfd_scraper raises ImportError (DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE False), the dual_names branch at line 602 is skipped and canonical_key falls back to clean_series_name(raw_name) = 'penguin tucnak' (space, NO pipe). merge_dual_canonical_series (line 247) only acts on keys containing '|', so the pipe-less fallback key never triggers the dual merge. Confirmed end-to-end with files [The Penguin S01E01, Tucnak S01E02, The Penguin - Tucnak S01E03]: with csfd PRESENT result['series'] = {'penguin'} (1 group); with csfd ABSENT result['series'] = {'penguin','tucnak'} (2 separate groups, the 'penguin tucnak' raw key gets partially collapsed by substring/word merges but the two single-name series stay split). group_movies has the identical structure at line 799-807.
findings.trigger_input: Result set containing 'The Penguin S01E01.mkv', 'Tucnak S01E02.mkv', 'The Penguin - Tucnak S01E03.mkv' with csfd_scraper not importable (or import failure).
findings.wrong_output: Two separate series 'penguin' and 'tucnak' instead of one merged 'penguin' series; episodes scattered across two list entries. Grouping/dedup result depends on presence of an optional dependency rather than on the data.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py gates the dual-name canonical key on the optional module:
L24-28: `try: from csfd_scraper import create_canonical_from_dual_names; DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE = True\nexcept ImportError: DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE = False`
L588: `canonical_key = series` (= ep_info['series_name'] = clean_series_name(raw_name))
L602-608: `if dual_names and DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE:` ... `canonical_key = dual_result['canonical_key']` — the pipe-joined key is ONLY produced when DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE is True; when False the whole block is skipped and the space-joined default key survives.
merge_dual_canonical_series only acts on pipe keys: L247 `dual_keys = [k for k in series.keys() if '|' in k]` — a pipe-less fallback key never enters this merge.
merge_substring_series leaves an orphan: L156-166 iterates keys_to_merge; the first pair folds 'penguin tucnak' into 'penguin' and `del series[long_key]` (L166), so the second pair ('tucnak','penguin tucnak') hits the guard L157 `if short_key not in series or long_key not in series: continue` and 'tucnak' stays a separate series.
group_movies has the same gate: L799 `if movie_info['dual_names'] and DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE:`.
findings.repro:
Input files (series): 'The Penguin S01E01.mkv', 'Tucnak S01E02.mkv', 'The Penguin - Tucnak S01E03.mkv'.

Parsing keys (verified via `python3 -c`): the three default canonical_keys are 'penguin', 'tucnak', 'penguin tucnak' (extract_dual_names('The Penguin - Tucnak') == ('The Penguin','Tucnak'); clean_series_name of the dual raw name == 'penguin tucnak', NO pipe).

End-to-end (ran group_by_series under conftest Kodi mocks, reloading lib.grouping with csfd_scraper stubbed present vs sys.modules['csfd_scraper']=None):
- DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE=True  -> result['series'] keys == ['penguin'], total_episodes=3  (1 merged group). EXPECTED.
- DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE=False -> result['series'] keys == ['penguin','tucnak'], with penguin total_episodes=2 (S01E01+S01E03) and tucnak total_episodes=1 (S01E02). ACTUAL WRONG: one logical series split into two list entries; the dual file's episode lands under 'penguin' and 'Tucnak' episodes are stranded.

group_movies analog ('The Penguin 2024.mkv','Tucnak 2024.mkv','The Penguin - Tucnak 2024.mkv'): present-> ['penguin|2024','tucnak|2024']; absent-> ['penguin tucnak|2024','penguin|2024','tucnak|2024'] (extra split). So movie grouping is also presence-dependent, though its merge logic differs from the series path.

Trigger requires csfd_scraper to be non-importable. In current builds it IS shipped (build_zip.sh excludes only listed paths, not csfd_scraper.py) and imports cleanly (all heavy deps wrapped in try/except), so DUAL_NAMES_AVAILABLE is normally True. The bug manifests only on stripped/partial installs missing csfd_scraper.py, or pre-fix builds that excluded it (cf. commit 'fix: include csfd_scraper.py in repository builds'). Hence real but environmental, not data-driven.

findings.title: Catastrophic (quadratic) regex backtracking on filenames containing long runs of separator characters causes multi-second to minute-scale hangs.
findings.mechanism: Patterns of the form ^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]+... combine a lazy .+? with a separator character class. On input that is a long run of separator chars with no terminating digit/marker, the engine backtracks O(n^2). Measured via the public parse_episode_info: a 3.5KB 'Movie Title - . - . ...end' filename = ~620ms; raw _PATTERN_0x00 scales 251ms(n=1000) -> 940ms(n=2000) -> 3099ms(n=4000), clearly quadratic. group_by_series calls parse_movie_info + parse_episode_info on every file, across up to 20 pages x 500 files, so a few crafted/decorative filenames stall the whole grouping pass and freeze the Kodi UI.
findings.trigger_input: A Webshare filename with a long separator run and no episode/year token, e.g. 'Movie Title' + ' - . '*700 + 'end' (decorative dashes/dots are common on file hosts).
findings.wrong_output: parse_episode_info hangs ~0.6s on one 3.5KB name and grows quadratically; a handful in one result set yields seconds-to-minutes of UI freeze during search grouping.
findings.evidence:
parsing.py lines 21-34 define the vulnerable patterns, all built as lazy-prefix + greedy-separator-class with no token forcing forward progress:
  _PATTERN_S00E00  = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]+[\(\[]?[Ss](\d{1,2})[Ee](\d{1,3})[\)\]]?')   (line 21)
  _PATTERN_0x00    = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]+[\(\[]?(\d{1,2})x(\d{1,3})[\)\]]?')           (line 23)
  _PATTERN_ABSOLUTE_EP = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s\.\-]+(?:ep?\.?\s*)?(\d{1,3})(?!\d)', re.I)      (line 25)
  _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR  = re.compile(r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]*[\(\[]?((?:19|20)\d{2})(?!x\d{3,4})...')  (line 34)
grouping.py runs BOTH parsers on every file with no length cap: parse_movie_info(filename) (line 573), parse_episode_info(filename) (line 579), and parse_movie_info(file_dict['name']) in group_movies (line 789). fetch_and_group_series feeds up to max_pages=20 x limit=500 files into group_by_series (lines 1247, 1335). No filename-length cap exists anywhere (grep for [:255]/maxlen/len(filename) in lib/*.py returned nothing). _filter_irrelevant (grouping.py line 66) only drops zero-overlap files, so a crafted name containing the query word passes through; browse mode (falsy search_query) or filter_irrelevant=False bypasses it entirely.
findings.repro:
Trigger filename: 'Movie Title' + ' - . '*700 + 'end' (3514 chars, no SxxExx/NxN/year token).
Observed (python3 from repo root):
  parse_episode_info, n=700  (3514 chars): 530.9ms ; n=1400 (7014 chars): 2162.3ms  -> clean O(n^2)
  Per-pattern raw .match() scaling, n=1000/2000/4000:
    _PATTERN_0x00:    326 / 1315 / 5671 ms
    _PATTERN_S00E00:  300 / 1071 / 4681 ms
    _PATTERN_ABSOLUTE_EP: 557 / 1848 / 8260 ms
    _PATTERN_MOVIE_YEAR:  270 / 1084 / 4955 ms
  Mechanism proof: core r'^(.+?)[\s_\.\-]+(\d{1,2})x(\d{1,3})' on the sep run = 234/791/2911ms (quadratic), but the SAME pattern on a 4000-char non-separator run 'T '+'a'*4000+' end' = 0.07ms -> blowup is specific to long [\s_\.\-] runs with no terminating digit token.
  Realistic-length check: at 255/512/1024 chars cost is only 3.8 / 14.8 / 64.8 ms (both parses). 5 crafted 2KB files in one result set = 1291ms. A file containing the query word survives _filter_irrelevant: parse cost on 'Matrix'+' - . '*700+'end' = 782ms.
Expected output: parse_episode_info should return None quickly (the name has no episode marker); actual = correct None value but only after 0.5-2+ s of CPU per name, freezing the synchronous Kodi grouping pass.

findings.title: Surviving canonical_key is non-deterministic across fetches: it depends on file/dict insertion order, so the same data can produce different series keys.
findings.mechanism: merge_word_order_series uses target = keys[0] (line 201-202) where keys come from word_set_map populated by iterating series.keys() in insertion order. Insertion order follows the order files arrive from the API. Confirmed: files [South Park S01E01, Park South S02E01] -> surviving key 'south park'; the SAME files reversed -> surviving key 'park south'. The canonical_key is used downstream as the series identity (file_dict['series_name'], state keys, favorite matching). So a re-fetch with a different API ordering changes the key the favorites/state layer must match against, causing avoidable drift (display_name stays stable via pick_best_display_name_from_list, but the key does not).
findings.trigger_input: Two series sharing the same word set in different order across episodes, e.g. 'South Park S01E01.mkv' and 'Park South S02E01.mkv', re-fetched in a different result order.
findings.wrong_output: canonical_key for the merged series flips between 'south park' and 'park south' depending purely on input ordering within a single fetch.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py merge_word_order_series picks the surviving key by raw insertion order:
  L189-194: `for key in series.keys(): ... word_set_map[ws_key].append(key)`
  L201-202: `# Use first key as target` / `target = keys[0]`
Insertion order = file order: group_by_series L618 `if canonical_key not in result['series']:` (first-seen wins) iterating `files`; fetch_and_group_series builds order via L1311 `all_files.extend(page_files)` / L1269 — i.e. raw API/page order.
The surviving key is used as durable identity:
  grouping.py L645 `file_dict['series_name'] = canonical_key`
  favorites.py L73 `(t, _normalize_canonical(t, entry.get('canonical_key')))`; keys.py L32-34 `if not series or '|' not in series: return series` — NO pipe ⇒ "south park" and "park south" normalize to THEMSELVES (do not collapse).
  state.py L130/L170 `"ep:{0}|...".format(_normalize_series_key(series), ...)` — playback state keys on the same non-collapsing normalized key, with NO display-name fallback.
Mitigation that downgrades severity: favorites_ui.py L184-187 `find_favorite_by_name(t, entry.get('display_name'))` bridges via display_name, and display_name is stable here (pick_best_display_name_from_list tiebreak `(-count, len, alpha)` returns "Park South" for both orderings).
findings.repro:
Trigger files in one query result: 'South Park S01E01.mkv' and 'Park South S02E01.mkv' (same word set, different order).

Parser (ran): parse_episode_info('South Park S01E01.mkv')['series_name'] == 'south park'; parse_episode_info('Park South S02E01.mkv')['series_name'] == 'park south'; get_word_set_key both == 'park south' (same set).

Merge target selection (ran, replicating L189-202):
  insertion order ['south park','park south'] -> surviving key 'south park'
  reversed       ['park south','south park'] -> surviving key 'park south'
So the SAME data, re-fetched in different API order, yields a different canonical_key.

Durable-layer impact (ran):
  normalize_series_key('south park') != normalize_series_key('park south')  -> True (do NOT collapse)
  state key 'ep:south park|s1e1'  vs  'ep:park south|s1e1'  -> NOT EQUAL
Expected: stable identity across fetches. Actual: playback watched/resume markers written under the pre-flip key are orphaned after a re-fetch flips the surviving key (no display-name fallback in state.py).

Favorites toggle specifically is NOT broken in this case: pick_best_display_name_from_list(['South Park','Park South']) == pick_best_display_name_from_list(['Park South','South Park']) == 'Park South' (stable), and find_favorite_by_name matches on that stable display_name. So the favorites harm the candidate emphasizes is mostly guarded; the unguarded harm is playback-state drift.

findings.title: quality_score is never used to sort versions; dedupe ordering is by size only, contradicting the documented 'quality score ranks dupes' contract.
findings.mechanism: All version-list sorts in grouping.py sort by int(size) desc (lines 697, 732-734, 831, 904, 1113, 1227). parse_quality_metadata's quality_score (parsing.py 54-135) is computed only lazily in series_ui.py (lines 246, 316) and used solely to build the display label via _build_version_metadata; show_version_dialog/select_movie_version never re-sort by quality_score, so dialog order = size order. CLAUDE.md and parse_quality_metadata's docstring claim quality_score (0-125) ranks duplicate versions, but it has no effect on ordering — a smaller-but-higher-quality file (e.g. efficient x265 1080p) ranks below a larger lower-quality file (bloated x264 1080p).
findings.trigger_input: An episode/movie with two versions where the higher-quality_score file is smaller, e.g. 'Show.S01E01.1080p.BluRay.x265.mkv' (score ~100, 2GB) vs 'Show.S01E01.1080p.WEBRip.x264.mkv' (score ~83, 4GB).
findings.wrong_output: The 4GB lower-score x264 version is listed first/'best' purely because it is larger; quality_score is dead weight for ranking.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py — every version sort uses size, never quality_score:
L697: `deduplicated.sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size', 0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)`
L732-734: `episodes[ep_num].sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size', 0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)`
L831, L904, L923, L991, L1113, L1227: identical size-DESC sort lambdas.
The L687 comment lies: `# Sort versions by quality score (highest first)` immediately precedes the size sort at L697.

parse_quality_metadata docstring (parsing.py L51): `'quality_score': int # 0-125 ranking (higher = better quality)` and L43 "for ranking duplicates" — but `grep quality_score lib/ --exclude parsing.py` returns EMPTY: the field is never read by any consumer.

series_ui.py L246/L316 only stash `v['quality_meta'] = parse_quality_metadata(...)`; ui.py `_build_version_metadata` (L31-52) reads only quality_meta['quality'/'source'/'codec'/'audio'] for the label — never 'quality_score'. show_version_dialog (L259) and select_movie_version (L328) call `dialog.select(... listitems ...)` iterating `versions` in grouping's size order; neither re-sorts.
findings.repro:
python3 -c on the candidate's filenames:
  'Show.S01E01.1080p.BluRay.x265.mkv' (2GB) -> quality_score=100
  'Show.S01E01.1080p.WEBRip.x264.mkv' (4GB) -> quality_score=83
Replicating grouping.py L697 sort (size DESC):
  size-sorted order (dialog order, best=first): ['B', 'A']   <- 4GB x264 listed first as "best"
  quality_score-sorted order (documented contract): ['A', 'B']  <- x265 BluRay should be first
Expected (per docstring/CLAUDE.md): higher quality_score ranks first -> A (BluRay x265).
Actual: B (larger WEBRip x264) is first. quality_score has zero effect on ordering; it is dead weight for ranking, used only to build the display label.

findings.title: Spinoff-protection guard reads stale pre-merge total_episodes, so a transitive chain over-merges distinct spinoffs that a direct comparison would have kept separate.
findings.mechanism: keys_to_merge is collected in one pass (lines 128-153) reading series[short_key]['total_episodes'] and series[long_key]['total_episodes'] BEFORE any merge happens. The guard `if min(short_eps, long_eps) >= 3 and all(len(w) <= 5 for w in extra_words): continue` is meant to keep distinct short-suffix spinoffs apart. With an intermediate key present, the short key's count is captured small (e.g. 2) at collect time. Merges are then performed (lines 156-166) and the short key grows (absorbs the intermediate), but the already-collected pair for the larger spinoff still uses the stale count, so its guard passed (min(2,3)=2<3) and the merge proceeds — whereas the equivalent direct pair (post-merge counts 4 vs 3) would be blocked.
findings.trigger_input: Three series with subset names where the shortest is a substring of both others and all extra words are <=5 chars, e.g. files for 'Dragon Ball S01E01-02', 'Dragon Ball Z S01E01-02', 'Dragon Ball Z GT S01E01-03' (keys 'dragon ball', 'dragon ball z', 'dragon ball z gt').
findings.wrong_output: All three collapse into a single 'dragon ball' series (7 episodes). Without the intermediate 'dragon ball z' present the direct 'dragon ball' vs 'dragon ball z gt' pair is correctly kept separate, so the result is inconsistent and merges distinct shows.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py merge_substring_series collects all pairs in one pass reading pre-merge counts, then mutates:
L127-153 (collect): `short_eps = series[short_key]['total_episodes']` / `long_eps = series[long_key]['total_episodes']` then guard `if min(short_eps, long_eps) >= 3 and all(len(w) <= 5 for w in extra_words): continue` — counts are read BEFORE any merge.
L155-166 (apply): `merge_season_data(series[short_key], series[long_key])` ... `del series[long_key]` — this grows short_key's total_episodes, but already-queued pairs keep their stale guard decision.
merge_season_data L460-465 recomputes `target['total_episodes'] = len(unique_episodes)` only on the target being merged, never re-evaluating queued guards.
The skip guard at L156-158 only protects against a key already deleted, not against a stale count: `if short_key not in series or long_key not in series: continue`.
findings.repro:
Input files (parsed keys verified via parse_episode_info): "Dragon Ball S01E01.mkv"+"...E02" -> key 'dragon ball' (2 eps); "Dragon Ball Z S01E03.mkv"+"...E04" -> 'dragon ball z' (2 eps, distinct ep numbers); "Dragon Ball Z GT S01E01.mkv"+"E02"+"E03" -> 'dragon ball z gt' (3 eps).
Collect pass (counts db=2, dbz=2, dbzgt=3) yields keys_to_merge = [('dragon ball','dragon ball z'), ('dragon ball','dragon ball z gt'), ('dragon ball z','dragon ball z gt')] — the (db, dbzgt) guard min(2,3)=2<3 passes so the merge is queued.
Apply pass: merge 'dragon ball z' (eps 3,4) into 'dragon ball' -> grows to 4 eps; then the stale-queued (db, dbzgt) merge still fires, absorbing GT.
Observed result: {'dragon ball': total_episodes=4} — all three collapse into one series.
Counterfactual (no intermediate, db already 4 eps vs dbzgt 3 eps): keys_to_merge = [] (guard min(4,3)=3>=3 with extra words 'gt'<=5 blocks) -> result keeps {'dragon ball','dragon ball z gt'} separate.
Both runs executed via standalone simulation of L108-168 + merge_season_data; outputs printed above. The merge outcome is therefore inconsistent and over-merges the distinct GT spinoff.

findings.title: Movie-exclusion block does unguarded version['ident'] and f['ident'], so a movie file lacking an 'ident' key produces duplicate listings (file shown as both grouped movie and raw non_series item)
findings.mechanism: Lines 750-758 build grouped_movie_idents via `version['ident']` (753) then filter non_series via `f['ident']` (757). Both are direct subscript, not .get(). todict() omits the 'ident' key entirely when the API <file> has no <ident> child (removed/edge entries under maybe_removed=true). result['movies'] is already assigned on line 747 BEFORE the KeyError fires on 753, and the surrounding try/except (761) swallows the KeyError — so the non_series exclusion never executes. The identical file ends up listed both as a grouped movie AND in the flat non_series list.
findings.trigger_input: A movie-pattern file with no ident: <file><name>Inception.2010.1080p.mkv</name><size>500</size></file> (todict -> {'name':...,'size':...}, no 'ident').
findings.wrong_output: Same movie appears twice in the UI — once under its grouped 'Inception (2010)' entry and once as a standalone non_series row — instead of being deduplicated.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py lines 745-763:
```
movies_result = group_movies(result['non_series'])
result['movies'] = movies_result['movies']            # 747: set BEFORE the crash

grouped_movie_idents = set()
for movie_data in movies_result['movies'].values():
    for version in movie_data['versions']:
        grouped_movie_idents.add(version['ident'])     # 753: KeyError if dict lacks 'ident'

result['non_series'] = [
    f for f in result['non_series']
    if f['ident'] not in grouped_movie_idents          # 757: also unguarded subscript
]
except Exception as e:                                  # 761: swallows the KeyError
    log_debug(f"Error grouping movies: {e}")
```
todict (utils.py:129-138) only sets keys for child elements present: `for e in xml: ... result[e.tag]=value` — so a <file> with no <ident> child yields a dict with no 'ident' key (not None). deduplicate_versions (grouping.py:492 `ident = v.get('ident')`) keeps such a file as a movie version. The movie-pattern file reaches non_series via grouping.py:684-685 (parse_movie_info matches, parse_episode_info returns None).
findings.repro:
Input file (no <ident>): `<file><name>Inception.2010.1080p.mkv</name><size>500</size></file>`.
Verified: parse_movie_info('Inception.2010.1080p.mkv') -> {'is_movie': True,'title':'inception','year':2010,...}; parse_episode_info -> None (so it goes to non_series). todict on that <file> -> {'name':'Inception.2010.1080p.mkv','size':'500'} with 'ident' absent.
Ran the exact lines 747-763 logic: result['movies'] set on 747, then `version['ident']` on 753 raised KeyError('ident'), caught on 761, so the non_series rebuild (755-758) never ran.
Observed output:
  movies present : ['inception|2010']
  non_series kept: ['Inception.2010.1080p.mkv']
  DUPLICATED?    : True
Expected: file appears ONLY under grouped movie 'Inception (2010)'. Actual: appears both as grouped movie AND as a standalone non_series row in the UI.

findings.title: Name+size dedup key (name, size) is unhashable when size is a list, raising TypeError that aborts group_by_series
findings.mechanism: When a version has no usable ident (missing/empty/'unknown'), the fallback builds `key = (name, size)` and adds it to a set (lines 502-508). If todict() produced size as a list (duplicated <size> tags) the tuple is unhashable -> TypeError on `seen_name_size.add(key)`. deduplicate_versions is called at line 693 and 731 in the unguarded main body of group_by_series, so the exception propagates and aborts the whole grouping.
findings.trigger_input: Episode file with no ident and duplicated size tags: <file><name>Show S01E01.mkv</name><size>100</size><size>200</size></file> (todict -> name set, ident absent, size=['100','200']).
findings.wrong_output: TypeError 'unhashable type: list' propagates out of group_by_series; search result rendering fails entirely.
findings.repro:
Trigger file dict (as todict produces from `<file><name>Show S01E01.mkv</name><size>100</size><size>200</size></file>`, no ident): {'name': 'Show S01E01.mkv', 'size': ['100','200']}.

Ran end-to-end with xbmc stubbed:
  from lib.grouping import group_by_series
  group_by_series([{'name':'Show S01E01.mkv','size':['100','200']}], token=None, enable_csfd=False, search_query='show')
Observed output: `EXCEPTION (propagated out of group_by_series): TypeError - unhashable type: 'list'`.
Expected: a result dict with the episode grouped under series (or at worst the file routed to non_series), not a propagated exception.

Also verified todict in isolation: ET.fromstring('<file><name>Show S01E01.mkv</name><size>100</size><size>200</size></file>') -> {'name': 'Show S01E01.mkv', 'size': ['100', '200']} (size type list). And deduplicate_versions([{'name':'Show S01E01.mkv','size':['100','200']}]) alone -> TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' (a single list-size version with no ident is enough; no duplicate needed). Note line 697 `int(v.get('size',0))` would also raise on a list, so the gap is broader than just the dedup key.

findings.title: Non-numeric size string crashes the entire group_by_series sort (ValueError), losing all series grouping for the query
findings.mechanism: The size-sort key is `int(v.get('size', 0)) if v.get('size') else 0`. The guard only catches falsy values (None/empty). `size` comes verbatim from XML via todict() (e.api.py `e.text`), so any truthy non-digit text (e.g. a float string '500.0', a value with stray whitespace/comma, or any malformed `<size>`) makes int() raise ValueError. Unlike lib/utils.py line 236 which guards with `.isdigit()`, this sort does not. The exception propagates out of the per-episode loop and aborts group_by_series. Same unguarded pattern repeats at lines 697,733,832,905,924,992,1114,1228.
findings.trigger_input: A WebShare result whose <size> is non-integer text, e.g. file dict {'name':'Show S01E01.mkv','size':'5000.0','ident':'X'} (or '5 000', or 'abc'). One such file in any episode/movie group.
findings.wrong_output: ValueError raised mid-sort; group_by_series throws; series view shows nothing / falls back to ungrouped or errors for that whole search.
findings.evidence:
grouping.py:697 (and identical at 733, 832, 905, 924, 992, 1114, 1228):
  deduplicated.sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size', 0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)
The guard only catches falsy size; any truthy non-decimal string raises ValueError. Contrast utils.py:236 which guards: `'size' in file and file['size'].isdigit()`.
Propagation: line 697 is inside group_by_series's post-loop with NO try/except; group_by_series→fetch_and_group_series→dosearch (search_ui.py:77, NOT inside the try at 73-76 which only wraps int(xml.find('total').text))→router→ caught only at main.py:20 `except Exception` (logs, leaves directory empty, never reaches endOfDirectory at search_ui.py:115).
todict (utils.py:131): `value = ... if len(e)>0 else e.text` — size comes verbatim from XML e.text.
findings.repro:
Sort key in isolation (exact expression), python3:
  versions=[{'name':'Show S01E01.mkv','size':'5000.0','ident':'X'},{'name':'b','size':'4000','ident':'Y'}]
  versions.sort(key=lambda v: int(v.get('size',0)) if v.get('size') else 0, reverse=True)
  => ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '5000.0'
Also '5 000','5,000','abc' all raise ValueError; a list (duplicate <size> tag via todict) raises TypeError. Expected: graceful sort; Actual: exception aborts group_by_series, series view shows nothing (empty directory).
BUT trigger not observed: across 17,209 real <size> values in tests/ fixtures, every value is int()-parseable (only anomaly -1820535148 parses fine); no empty/whitespace/duplicate <size>. WebShare emits size as a plain integer byte count, and the `else 0` already handles empty <size></size> (e.text=None → falsy → 0). So crash requires WebShare to violate its integer-size contract, which is unobserved.

findings.title: A series/movie whose real name contains a quality/codec/format token (HD, 4K, AAC, AC3, DTS) has that word deleted from the display name
findings.mechanism: clean_name() unconditionally applies _RE_QUALITY (480p|720p|1080p|2160p|4K|UHD|FHD|HD) and _RE_CODEC (x264|...|AAC|AC3|DTS|DD5.1|Atmos|TrueHD) word-boundary subs to the display candidate. These regexes do not distinguish a metadata tag from a title word, so any title word equal to one of these tokens is stripped. The returned value is the CLEANED string (best_cleaned at line 421), never the original, so the displayed name is permanently wrong.
findings.trigger_input: Episodes like 'Aac Show S01E01.mkv' -> 'Show'; '4K The Movie S01E01.mkv' -> 'The Movie'; a movie raw_title 'HD' or a series literally named with 'HD'/'4K'.
findings.wrong_output: Display name 'Aac Show' -> 'Show'; '4K The Movie' -> 'The Movie' (title word silently removed).
findings.evidence:
grouping.py defines the cleaning regexes that include bare title-like tokens:
  line 40: `_RE_QUALITY = re.compile(r'\b(480p|720p|1080p|2160p|4K|UHD|FHD|HD)\b', re.IGNORECASE)`
